title: Smart Underwear Counts Your Farts — For Science!
summary: Have you ever wondered how many farts a day is normal? Well, scientists finally have an answer — and it's all thanks to some smart underwear! A team at the University of Maryland created a small sensor, about the size of a quarter, that clips onto your undies and measures the hydrogen in your farts. Hydrogen is a gas made by tiny microbes in your gut when they digest food. The idea started when researcher Brantley Hall got frustrated with lab tests. He stuck a sensor down his own pants, let one rip, and the signal was huge! That led to the "smart underwear" project. In a study with college students, the sensor found that people fart an average of 32 times a day. But it varied a lot — some as few as 4 times, others up to 59! When they ate high-fiber gumdrops, almost everyone tooted more. Hall was shocked that doctors don't know a normal fart number. "If you go to the doctor, they don't know the normal number of farts," he says. So his team launched the Human Flatus Atlas (flatus is a fancy word for fart). Volunteers wear the sensor all day and night, even during sports like rugby — but no biking, because the seat hits the sensor. They also found three farting types: "zen digesters" who rarely fart, "hydrogen hyperproducers" who fart a lot, and "normal people" in between. The project is so popular that 800 sensors flew out in days, and over 3,500 people want to join. So next time you toot, remember — you're helping science!
why_it_matters: Learning about your fart patterns can tell doctors if your tummy is working well, so you can avoid stomach aches and feel better during school and play.

keywords.explanation: A small device that can detect something like temperature, movement, or gas.
A light gas that has no color or smell and is made in our gut when food breaks down.
Tiny living things, like bacteria, that live in our stomach and help digest food.
The scientific word for the gas released when you fart.
A sport where two teams run, pass, and tackle to carry an oval ball over a line.
People whose bodies process food in a certain way — here, used as a fun name for fart groups.

questions.question: What does the smart underwear sensor measure?
How many times a day did the study find people fart on average?
Who created the smart underwear sensor?
options: A team at the University of Maryland
A group of students in Japan
questions.correct_answer: A team at the University of Maryland

questions.question: What happened when people ate high-fiber gumdrops?
What activity was NOT allowed while wearing the sensor?
What is the Human Flatus Atlas?
options: A project collecting data on farts from many people
A new type of bicycle seat
questions.correct_answer: A project collecting data on farts from many people

background_read: The University of Maryland is a large public university in the eastern United States where scientists study health and technology.
Brantley Hall is the researcher who got frustrated with lab tests and first stuck a sensor down his pants to measure fart gas.
Gut microbes are the billions of tiny organisms living in your intestines that break down food, often producing gas as a byproduct.
The Human Flatus Atlas is a scientific project that aims to build a database of normal and unusual fart patterns across different people.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researcher Brantley Hall and a team at the University of Maryland.
WHAT: They created a smart underwear sensor that counts farts by measuring hydrogen gas and learned that people fart about 32 times a day on average.
WHERE: In a study with college students, likely at the university in the United States.
WHY: Because doctors didn't know what a normal number of farts was, and this info could help understand gut health.

perspectives.description: The sensor could help doctors quickly see if someone has a gut problem without painful tests.
Some people might feel embarrassed or shy about wearing a device that tracks their farts all day.
The study so far used mostly college students, so scientists need more volunteers of different ages and diets to get a complete picture.
